title: Kladdkaka
subtitle: Swedish sticky chocolate cake, intentionally underbaked
excerpt: A Swedish sticky chocolate cake with a set surface and a gooey, brownie-like interior. Mixed in one bowl with melted butter and cocoa, baked at high heat for a short time. The underdoneness is intentional: the centre never sets fully, which is what makes it kladdkaka.
sourceNotes: Kladdkaka (literally 'sticky cake' or 'gooey cake') is a Swedish home-baking staple with documented roots in twentieth-century Swedish domestic cookery. It appears in Swedish cookbooks from the mid-twentieth century and has been a fixture of café culture in Sweden since at least the 1970s. The recipe's essential characteristics (a deliberately underbaked, fudgy centre made from butter, sugar, eggs, flour, and cocoa) are consistent across the Swedish domestic tradition. The recipe here draws on the proportions recorded in Swedish domestic handbooks and the classic café version: unadorned, served with a dusting of icing sugar and a spoonful of whipped cream.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in the tin, unmoulded and undusted. Thaw in the fridge overnight; bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bakes well the day before and the texture improves as it chills. Keep in the tin in the fridge and dust with icing sugar just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 155°C. The high heat sets the surface and edges while the centre stays liquid; the timing is tight — 13 minutes is gooey, 17 minutes is more set. Pull it at the point that suits you. Domestic ovens drift; an oven thermometer is worth using.

glossaryTerms.definition: A kladdkaka is ready when the surface is matte and set — no wet-looking patches — but the whole cake wobbles as one mass when you shake the tin. The wobble is the gooey interior, still liquid beneath the set surface. If the centre does not wobble, the cake is overbaked and will cool to a dry, fudge-brownie texture rather than the intended sticky one.

content.text: Kladdkaka is one of the most forgiving cakes to make and one of the most unforgiving to judge when to take out of the oven. The difference between correctly underbaked and overbaked is about 3 to 4 minutes. The correct
is a matte, set surface with a single-mass wobble beneath. The recipe is otherwise straightforward: everything melted together, no creaming, no folding technique required.

Heat the oven to 175°C conventional. Grease a 23 cm springform tin and line the base with baking paper. Melt the butter in a medium saucepan over low heat. Remove from the heat and stir in the caster sugar and cocoa powder until smooth. Add the eggs and vanilla and beat vigorously until combined. Sift over the flour and salt and fold in until just smooth. Pour into the prepared tin; the batter is quite liquid.

Bake for 13 to 17 minutes depending on how gooey you want the centre. At 13 minutes, the edges are set and the centre is liquid beneath a surface skin; at 17 minutes, the whole cake sets to a dense, fudgy texture. The safe zone for most people is 15 minutes: surface matte and set, whole cake wobbling as one mass, edges just pulling away from the tin. Allow to cool in the tin for at least 30 minutes before releasing the sides; the interior continues to set as it cools. Dust with icing sugar just before serving. Serve with whipped cream or vanilla ice cream.
